coredata.dc:title: Enhanced electrochemical advanced oxidation on boride activated carbon: The influences of boron groups
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xu
Wang, Lida
Sun, Wen
Yang, Zhengqing
Jin, Jingjing
You, Danni
Liu, Guichang
coredata.dc:description: Exploring an efficient electro-catalyst is fundamentally important to enhance degradation of persistent organic pollutants during electrochemical advanced oxidation process. In this work, boride activated carbon (BAC) containing different boron group content was prepared as low-cost and efficient catalysts. BAC possessed a more positive onset potential of oxygen reduction reaction (0.458 V vs. RHE) and a faster rate of H2O2 generation (365 mg/L pH=3), compared with activated carbon (0.425 V vs. RHE, 138 mg/L pH=3). DFT calculations and XPS results revealed that -BC3 and -BC2O active groups enhanced oxygen adsorption, and small electronegativity difference between B and O facilitated the desorption of •OOH. The rate of in-situ phenol degradation (0.193 min−1) increased by an order of magnitude as compared to activated carbon (0.018 min−1), because BAC promoted the in-situ conversion of H2O2 to •OH. Therefore, it provided a theoretical base for the further application of boron catalyst in two-electron oxygen reduction process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boride activated carbon
Electrochemical advanced oxidation
Boron groups
DFT calculation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21978036 ) and Major Project of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region Key Research and Development Program (NO. 2019BFG2016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM of (a) AC and (b) BAC. (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (d) pore distribution of catalysts.
(a) XRD (b) XPS (c) FT-IR of catalysts surface.

(a) CV curves of catalysts in oxygen-free (dash line) and oxygen-saturated (solid line) electrolyte. (b) polarization curves (subtract the background current in N2 atmosphere) with a rotating rate of 1600 rpm. (c) electron number and (d) H2O2 selectivity of catalysts. (pH=1, scanning rate=10 mV s−1).

(a) Performance and (d) current efficiency of H2O2 production on different catalysts. (initial pH value=3, potential=-0.2 V vs. RHE) (b) Performance and (e) current efficiency of H2O2 production at different potential (initial pH value=3). (c) Performance and (f) current efficiency of H2O2 production at different initial pH value.
B 1s spectra of (a) BAC-1, (b) BAC-2, (c) BAC-3, (d) BAC-4 and (e) BAC-5.
H2O2 production ration with content of -BC3 and -BC2O groups.
Adsorption models of O2 and intermediate •OOH on surface of graphene and different boron doping forms of carbon materials.

(a) Degradation of phenol on AC catalyst in oxygen-standard electrolyte, AC catalyst with 0.06 mM Fe2+ in oxygen-standard electrolyte, BAC catalyst in oxygen-free electrolyte and BAC catalyst in oxygen-standard electrolyte. (b) Influence of phenol degraded on BAC-3 with the addition of different tert-butyl alcohol concentration (c) Influence of potential on degradation of phenol. (d) Influence of initial pH value on degradation of phenol.(initial pH value=3, potential=-0.2 V vs. RHE)Influence of phenol degraded on AC catalyst in oxygen-standard electrolyte, AC catalyst with 0.06 mM Fe2+ in oxygen-standard electrolyte, BAC catalyst in oxygen-free electrolyte and BAC catalyst in oxygen-standard electrolyte. (b) Kinetic curves of phenol degraded on BAC-3 with the addition of different tert-butyl alcohol concentration (c) Kinetic curves of potential on degradation of phenol. (d) Kinetic curves of initial pH value on degradation of phenol.
Stability of phenol degraded by BAC-3 catalyst at -0.2 V vs. RHE and initial pH of 3.
Binding energy of O2 and intermediate •OOH and bond length of adsorbed O2 on surface of catalysts.

head.ce:title: Enhanced electrochemical advanced oxidation on boride activated carbon: The influences of boron groups

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, No. 2, Linggong Road, Dalian 116024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, No. 2, Linggong Road, Dalian 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dalian Key Laboratory of Flue Gas Purification and Waste Heat Utilization, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dalian Key Laboratory of Flue Gas Purification and Waste Heat Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDalian Key Laboratory of Flue Gas Purification and Waste Heat Utilization, Dalian 116024, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, No. 2, Linggong Road, Dalian 116024, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring an efficient electro-catalyst is fundamentally important to enhance degradation of persistent organic pollutants during electrochemical advanced oxidation process. In this work, boride activated carbon (BAC) containing different boron group content was prepared as low-cost and efficient catalysts. BAC possessed a more positive onset potential of oxygen reduction reaction (0.458 V vs. RHE) and a faster rate of H2O2 generation (365 mg/L pH=3), compared with activated carbon (0.425 V vs. RHE, 138 mg/L pH=3). DFT calculations and XPS results revealed that -BC3 and -BC2O active groups enhanced oxygen adsorption, and small electronegativity difference between B and O facilitated the desorption of •OOH. The rate of in-situ phenol degradation (0.193 min−1) increased by an order of magnitude as compared to activated carbon (0.018 min−1), because BAC promoted the in-situ conversion of H2O2 to •OH. Therefore, it provided a theoretical base for the further application of boron catalyst in two-electron oxygen reduction process.

ce:section.ce:para: Persistent organic pollutants (POPs), existing as a typical environmental pollutant, take on significant features including long-term residual, high toxicity, semi-volatile and high lipid solubility. It can be enriched and transferred in the food chain, which hazards human health and ecological environment. In recent years, advanced oxidation is regarded as the best method to degrade POPs [1]. It is worth noting that electro-Fenton (EF) technology, as a typical advanced oxidation method, overcomes the deficiencies of traditional oxidation method, such as easy decomposition and explosion during storage and transportation, which is paid critical attention [2].

EF reactions are defined as Eqs. (1)
and (
2
). Oxygen performs two-electron reduction on cathode (usually carbon-based catalysts) to produce H2O2 [3,4]. And due to the inferior oxidation performance of H2O2, additional substances (usually Fe2+) are usually required to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to form •OH, which is regarded as a strong oxidizing substance, and rapidly degrades POPs. At present, two main factors restrict the further development of EF technology.
(1)
O 2
+
H +
+ 2
e −
→
H 2
O 2
(2)
H 2
O 2
+ F
e
2 +
+
H +
→ F
e
3 +
+ · O H +
H 2
O

One of the limiting factors is the generation of H2O2, owing that more H2O2 provides more reactants for subsequent reactions. Oxygen reduction reaction is a competitive reaction. Inhibition of O2 reduction to produce H2O and promotion of O2 reduction to generate H2O2 can improve current efficiency. Therefore, a highly active and selective catalyst can significantly be desirable to EF. So far, the catalysts applied in the EF process mainly include platinum-based catalysts [5,6], transition metal catalysts [7,8], carbon-based catalysts [9–11]. Generally, platinum-based catalysts have the disadvantage of high price, and transition metal catalysts present poor performance after long term usage on account of metal leaching [12]. While carbon-based catalysts are brought into focus due to their high electrical conductivity and good stability. Whereas, serious problems such as lacking active site and poor selectivity still remain [13]. To settle these problems, a large number of elements (B [14], F [15], N [16], S [17]) have been used to modify carbon-based materials. Notably, activated carbon, as a kind of carbon-based material, is facile to obtain and owns a large specific surface area and rich pore structure, which can provide more active sites for oxygen reduction reaction.

The other factor is the ability to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to •OH [18]. In the traditional EF reaction, Fe2+ was regarded as Fenton reagent to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2. Meanwhile, Fe2+ is oxidized and then reduced continuously, so as to be reused as Fenton reagent. For the purpose of enhancing Fe3+/Fe2+ transformation ability, physical field assistance [19,20], addition of carboxylate complexes [21,22] and direct addition of reductant [23] are commonly adopted. However, it is still necessary to further explore these methods, owing that iron ions are precipitated at a high pH value, which restricts iron ions on recycling and catalyzing H2O2 to generate •OH. Typically, the problem can be figured out by the composite of carbon material and metal catalyst, but the stability of this catalyst was poor on account of metal leaching [24,25]. In addition, electrons are directly gained from carbon materials by constructing rich and poor electron centers on the surface of carbon materials [26]. Recently, it was reported that boron modified graphene had excellent properties [14]. The presence of boron could not only promote oxygen reduction to generate H2O2, but also decomposed H2O2 to produce •OH in the absence of metal, which was of great practical significance for expanding the application range of pH value in EF process. However, no further study revealed that how boron modification promoted the two-electron reaction of oxygen reduction reaction.

On the basis of previous studies, the boride activated carbon (BAC) was prepared by modifying commercial activated carbon. The obtained BAC was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), SEM energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR), N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and the electrocatalytic behavior of the electrode for oxygen reduction reaction was assessed by cyclic voltammograms (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and H2O2 production. Then, the influence of boron on oxygen reduction activity and selectivity was discussed by regulating the content and existing form of boron element, and the roles played by boron groups were jointly revealed by DFT calculation and experimental research. Finally, compared with the traditional EF process, the simulated phenol solution was degraded by BAC in in-situ metal-free EAOPs, and the effects of potential, pH value and stability on electrocatalytic performance were assessed as well.

The commercial activated carbon was purchased from Hefei Changlin Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd. China. Nitric acid (HNO3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and 4-Aminoantipyrine were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China. Boric acid (H3BO3), acetic acid (CH3COOH), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), ammonia (NH3•H2O) and phenol were obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China. Potassium ferricyanide (KFe(CN)4) was obtained from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China. Nafion solution (5 wt.%) was purchased from Dupont company. America. Carbon paper (HCP-330) was obtained from Shanghai Hesen Electrical Co., Ltd. China. All reagents were of analytical grade.

First of all, commercial activated carbon was stirred in 4 M HNO3 for 1 day to remove the impurities. After filtration with ultrapure water, the activated carbon was dried at 80°C to get pre-treated activated carbon, which was denoted as AC. 0.3 g of AC was stirred in the mixed solution containing H2O, H2SO4 and H3BO3 with the ratio of 59 mL: 1 mL: 2 g for 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 days, respectively. The obtained BAC was filtered and was vacuum dried at 80°C. The obtained BAC was denoted as BAC-x, where “x” indicated stirring time (count in days).
Electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 750E electrochemical workstation.

CV and RRDE measurement were performed in oxygen-free and oxygen-saturated electrolyte at a scanning rate of 10 mV s−1 (0.05 M H2SO4). The RRDE measurement was tested on an AFMSRCE instrument (Pine Research Instrumentation, USA) with Pt ring-glassy carbon disk electrode at a rotating speed of 1600 rpm. The ring potential was set to 1.3 V vs. RHE to oxidize H2O2 completely. N2/ O2 was pumped for 30 min to obtain an oxygen-free/oxygen-saturated environment. The catalyst ink was made by mixing 10 mg of catalysts, 1.5 mL of ultrapure water, 1.5 mL of ethanol and 0.05 mL of Nafion solution, and the catalyst ink was mixed uniformly after ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. 10 μL of catalyst ink was applied to the glassy carbon electrode (3 mm), Pt ring-glassy carbon (5.5 mm) disk electrode and glassy carbon disk electrode as the CV (EIS), RRDE and RDE measurements working electrode respectively, which dried at the room temperature. Platinum electrode and saturated calomel electrode served as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The electron transfer number (n) and hydrogen selectivity (H2O2%) of RRDE were calculated by the following Eqs. (3)
and (
4
).
(3)
n =
4
I d
I r
/ N +
I d
(4)
H 2
O 2
% =
200
I r
/ N
I r
/ N +
I d
Where
I r
and
I d
are ring current and disk current, respectively. N is collection coefficient, which was 0.38. The limiting current density for RDE was described by the Koutecky-Levich equation:
(5)
1 i
=
1
i k
+
1
0.62 n F A
D
2 / 3
ω
1 / 2
ν
− 1 / 6
c
Where i is a total current density,
i k
is the kinetic current density (in absence of any mass transfer effects), n is the number of transferred electrons, F is the Faraday constant, A is the rotating electrode area, D is the diffusion coefficient for H2O2, ω is the rotation velocity, ν is the kinematic viscosity of solution, and c is the H2O2 concentration.

3 mL of catalyst ink dripped onto the 5 cm2 of carbon paper and dried at room temperature as working electrode of H2O2 generation and pollutants degradation. The DSA with the same area and saturated calomel electrode were supplied as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The experiment was carried out in an undivided electrolytic cell (40 mL). The distance between the anode and cathode was 3 cm. In order to obtain the oxygen-saturated environment, the solution was aerated with high purity oxygen for 30 min before the trial. During the experiment, the velocity of high purity oxygen was maintained at 250 mL/min, and a magnetic stirrer was applied to keep the solution in a forced convection state. In H2O2 generation experiment, Na2SO4 (0.05 M) solution served as the test media, of which pH values were adjusted by H2SO4 (0.5 M). In the electrochemical oxidation experiment, the phenol (30 mg/L) was added as a pollutant. The above experiments were repeated at least twice to obtain the error line.

The current efficiency of electrocatalytic oxygen reduction to produce H2O2 was calculated by Eq. (6) .
(6)
C E =
n × F × c × V
∫
0
t
I d t
Where n is the number of transferred electrons. F is the Faraday constant. I is the current. V is the volume of electrolyte. t is the reaction time. c is the H2O2 concentration.

SEM and EDS were obtained on an America Nano SEM 450. XRD analysis was performed with a Shimadzu Lab XRD-7000s. Nitrogen-sorption isotherm was obtained on a micromeritics ASAP2460. XPS analysis was performed with a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB250Xi. FT-IR was obtained on an EQUINOX55. The concentrations of H2O2 and phenol were measured by an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash instruments Co., Ltd).

A stable orange complex was formed by H2O2 and Ti2+ in an acidic medium [27]. Therefore, H2O2 was quantitatively analyzed by titanium potassium oxalate method. 1 mL of solution to be measured, 1 mL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution and 1 mL of titanium potassium oxalate solution (0.2 M) were added into the volumetric flask respectively, and the volume was fixed to 25 mL by pure water. The NaAc-HAc buffer solution was maintained at the pH of 4.4. After 10 min, the solution was placed in a 10 mm colorimetric dish, which was measured at 375 nm.

Phenol was quantitatively analyzed by 4-aminoantipyrine method. Phenolic compounds reacted with 4-aminoantipyrine in the medium at the pH of 10 and the presence of K2[Fe(CN)4] to generate orange-red indoxyl antipyrine dyes [28,29]. The aqueous solution had the maximum absorption at 510 nm. 1 mL of the solution to be tested, 0.5 mL of NH4Cl-NH3•H2O buffer solution, 1 mL of 4-aminoantipyrine solution (20 g/L), 1 mL of K2[Fe(CN)4] solution (80 g/L) were added into the volumetric flask respectively, and the volume was fixed to 25 mL by pure water. The NH4Cl-NH3•H2O buffer solution was maintained at the pH of 10. After 10 min, the buffer solution was placed in a 10 mm colorimetric dish, which was measured at 510 nm.

The mechanism of H2O2 generated by BAC catalysts was calculated by Gaussian 09. The hybrid density functional theory was B3LYP 6-31g (d, p) [30–32]. Four basic graphene models were constructed respectively, including the initial carbon model, the carbon containing -BC3 groups model, the carbon containing -BC2O group model, and the carbon containing -BCO2 group model. The initial carbon model was composed of 54 carbon atoms, and hydrogen saturated was set at the carbon edge. The optimized models were shown in Fig. S1. The binding energies of O2 and •OOH on the surface of the models were calculated by Eq. (7) . Generally, the more negative binding energy means the more likely adsorption was to occur, and the more positive binding energy means the more easily desorption was to occur.
(7)
E ads
=
E
x + C
−
E x
−
E C
Where
E
x + C
was the energy of the adsorbed O2 or •OOH initial model, and
E x
was the energy of initial model.
E C
was the energy of O2 or •OOH.

As shown in Fig. 1
a and b, the surface morphology for the BAC catalysts was characterized by SEM analysis. Both the AC and BAC catalysts exhibited similar morphology with porous surfaces. The porous structure was further demonstrated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. Fig. 1
c showed that the catalysts were type IV isotherm curve. The unsaturated N2 uptake at high relative pressure (0.4∼0.8) and the sharp uptake at low relative pressure (<0.05) proved the existence of hierarchical pore structure [33]. As shown in Fig. 1
d, both AC and BAC catalysts were composed of micropores and mesoporous. And specific surface area, pore volume and pore size of BAC catalyst (1019.63 m2/g, 0.926 cm3/g, 5.702 nm) were slightly lower than those of AC catalyst (1074.74 m2/g, 0.961 cm3/g, 5.736 nm). This phenomenon might be relevant to collapse of carbon channels caused by acid modification [34].

The XRD pattern of AC catalyst displayed one broad peak at 26°, corresponding to the characteristic carbon (002) diffraction, as shown in Fig. 2
a. No other diffraction peaks were discovered on BAC catalysts, indicating that the crystal structure did not change after doping of boron. Only C and O elements were presented on AC catalyst, while three identified peaks corresponding to C, O and B elements were observed on BAC catalyst, as shown in Fig. 2
b, Table S1. The presence forms of boron were further analyzed by FT-IR ( Fig. 2
c). The B-C bending vibration appeared at 1121 nm [35], and the B-O stretching vibration appeared at 1410 nm on the surface of BAC catalysts [36], compared with AC catalysts.

The oxygen reduction activity and selectivity of BAC catalysts were measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE). Fig. 3
a showed the CV curves in oxygen-free and oxygen-saturated media at the pH of 1. AC and BAC catalysts exhibited higher current density in oxygen-saturated media than that in oxygen-free media, indicating that oxygen reduction reaction could occur on these catalysts in oxygen-saturated media. The onset potential in oxygen reduction reaction was a significant evaluation index for the performance of oxygen reduction. The onset potential in oxygen reduction reaction was 0.425 V vs. RHE (AC) and 0.458 V vs. RHE (BAC-3), respectively. Compared with AC catalyst, the prepared BAC-3 catalyst had more positive onset potential, reflecting the significant enhancement of electrochemical activity and the more facile electrocatalytic process of oxygen reduction reaction.

Fig. 3
b showed the polarization curves of AC and BAC catalysts in oxygen-saturated media of H2SO4 (0.05 M). The current densities in the disk electrode and ring electrode corresponded to O2 reduction and H2O2 oxidation, respectively. At the potential of -0.1 V vs. RHE, the current densities on BAC-1 (1.89 mA/cm2), BAC-2 (1.94 mA/cm2), BAC-3 (2.40 mA/cm2), BAC-4 (2.25 mA/cm2), BAC-5 (2.09 mA/cm2) were respectively higher than that on AC (1.62 mA/cm2) in oxygen reduction reaction, proving that BAC possessed better oxygen reduction activity. The transferred electron number and the H2O2 selectivity were calculated according to the current densities in disk electrode and ring electrode during the oxygen reduction process. As shown in Fig. 3
c, the average transferred electron numbers were calculated as 3.05 (AC), 2.74 (BAC-1), 2.57 (BAC-2), 2.44 (BAC-3), 2.47 (BAC-4) and 2.55 (BAC-5) at the potential of 0.2∼-0.3 V vs. RHE, respectively, indicating that the oxygen reduction reaction on BAC catalysts followed two-electron pathway. As shown in Fig. 3
d, the average H2O2 selectivity on BAC catalysts from high to low was 77.9% (BAC-3), 76.6% (BAC-4), 72.6% (BAC-5), 71.3% (BAC-2) and 63.0% (BAC-1), respectively, whereas the H2O2 selectivity on AC catalyst was only 47.3%. At the same time, H2O2 selectivity on BAC catalysts was superior to the commercial Pt/C catalyst [37,38]. As shown in Fig. S3, the kinetic current density of oxygen reduction reaction was calculated by Koutecky-Levich equation. The kinetic current density of BAC-3 (6.25 mA/cm2) was significantly higher than that of AC (2 mA/cm2) at 0.1 V vs. RHE, proving that BAC-3 had better oxygen reduction activity. As shown in Fig. S4, the EIS of AC and BAC catalysts was tested in an O2 saturated H2SO4 solution. BAC-3 had significantly lower charge-transfer resistance (72.4 ohm) and mass-transfer resistance than AC (158.1 ohm). The low charge-transfer resistance mass-transfer resistance was beneficial to oxygen reduction reaction. The above results demonstrated that BAC catalyst could improve oxygen reduction activity and H2O2 selectivity.

The production performance of H2O2 on AC and BAC was further evaluated by measuring H2O2 yield in a single compartment electrolytic cell under potentiostatic condition. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the concentration of H2O2 was 212 mg/L (BAC-1), 249 mg/L (BAC-2), 365 mg/L (BAC-3), 293 mg/L (BAC-4) and 268 mg/L (BAC-5) after 60 min, whereas the concentration of H2O2 generated by AC catalyst was only 138 mg/L at the potential of -0.2 V vs. RHE. As shown in Fig. 4
d, the current efficiency of oxygen redution reaction was calculated as 52.4% (AC), 60.3% (BAC-1), 68.3% (BAC-2), 78.4% (BAC-3), 73.6% (BAC-4) and 70.5% (BAC-5). The above results proved that the introduction of boron groups could improve the production performance of H2O2 and BAC-3 catalyst exhibited the highest H2O2 production rate, which was coincided with the results of the RRDE ( Fig. 3 ).

Among the BAC catalysts, BAC-3 catalyst had the highest H2O2 yield. Therefore, BAC-3 catalyst was supplied as cathode to investigate the influence of pH value and potential on the performance of H2O2 production. Fig. 4
b showed the curve of H2O2 concentration changed with time at different potentials. After 60 min, the H2O2 concentration was 183 mg/L (0.1 V vs. RHE), 264 mg/L (0 V vs. RHE), 291 mg/L (-0.1 V vs. RHE), 365 mg/L (-0.2 V vs. RHE), 441 mg/L (-0.3 V vs. RHE), 468 mg/L (-0.4 V vs. RHE). The yield of H2O2 significantly increased at a negative potential. This was because when the reaction was completely controlled by electrochemical reaction, more electrons could be supplied for the oxygen reduction reaction at a negative potential [39], and oxygen selectivity did not vary significantly with potential (Fig. S4). Fig. 4
e showed the current efficiency of H2O2 generated by oxygen reduction reaction at different potentials. The current efficiency was basically identical at the potential of 0.1 V∼-0.2 V vs. RHE, which were 79.2% (0.1 V vs. RHE), 78.5% (0 V vs. RHE), 78.8% (-0.1 V vs. RHE), 78.4% (-0.2 V vs. RHE), respectively. With the further increase of potential, the current efficiency was 73.1% (-0.3 V vs. RHE), 69.5% (-0.4 V vs. RHE), which decreased obviously, because of the side reaction of hydrogen evolution. The initial pH value significantly influenced the yield of H2O2, as shown in Fig. 4
c. As the initial pH value of the electrolyte increased, the yield of H2O2 generated by BAC-3 catalyst decreased, which was 528 mg/L (pH=1), 365 mg/L (pH=3), 277 mg/L (pH=5), 244 mg/L (pH=7). As shown in Fig. 4
f, the current efficiency was 79.3%, 78.4%, 73.5%, 71.6% at the initial pH value of 1, 3, 5, 7, respectively, and the current efficiency decreased with the increase of initial pH value. As reported, the proton-coupled electron transfer could be promoted by H+ during H2O2 production [39,40]. In a word, the BAC-3 catalyst had excellent oxygen reduction performance. As shown in Table S2, the H2O2 yield was 2.92 mg cm−2 h−1 at the initial pH value of 3 and the potential of -0.2 V vs. RHE, which was higher than that of the reported catalyst under the similar condition [14,41–45].

Notably, the H2O2 production performance of BAC catalysts was significantly higher than that of AC catalyst, in which BAC-3 catalyst exhibited the highest H2O2 production rate. It could be shown from the results above that the differences between AC catalyst and BAC catalyst were surface area, pore structure and elemental composition. In general, the catalysts with high surface area and rich pore structure, might provide more active sites for oxygen reduction reaction, thus reducing mass transfer resistance and improving oxygen reduction activity [46]. However, compared with AC catalyst, the surface area of BAC catalyst was smaller, but its oxygen reduction activity was higher, indicating that the surface area was not the main reason for the excellent oxygen reduction activity of BAC catalyst.

Thus, the enhancement of H2O2 production was owned to the introduction of boron element. The previous FT-IR results showed that boron elements existed in the form of B-C and B-O bond, so XPS was used to specifically analyzed the possible -BC3, -BC2O and -BCO2 groups, as shown in Figs. 5
and S5. No obvious boron peak was observed in AC catalyst. Therefore, the influence of boron groups content in BAC on oxygen reduction performance was investigated. BAC catalysts had three peaks at 189.0–189.3 eV, 190.3–190.5 eV and 191.7–191.9 eV, corresponding to -BC3, -BC2O and -BCO2 groups, respectively [36,47]. The production rate of H2O2 and the selectivity enhanced with the increase of modification time during the first 3 days, which was consisted with a rapid increase in boron over the previous three days ( Table 1
). The introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon plane destroyed the hexagonal structure of the carbon plane, resulting in the production of more defects and the enhancement of oxygen reduction activity [48,49]. As modification time continued to increase, H2O2 production rate and catalysts selectivity decreased slightly. Interestingly, it was speculated that the content of different boron groups influenced the oxygen reduction activity and selectivity, as shown in Fig. 6
. The catalyst with more BC3 and BC2O groups possessed higher oxygen reduction activity and selectivity, among which the total amounts of BC3 and BC2O groups were the highest in BAC-3 catalyst that had the most excellent oxygen reduction performance.

In order to further verify the above results, the DFT calculation was performed. As shown in Fig. 7
, the adsorption of O2 and •OOH on the surfaces of Gr, Gr-B, Gr-B-OH and Gr-B-2OH were constructed, respectively. The activation of O2 was the first step of oxygen reduction reaction and the key to improving the reactivity [50]. In general, the shorter O-O bond length was difficult to break, which was conducive to two-electron process. At the same time, the selectivity of H2O2 was controlled by the binding energy of •OOH on the catalyst surface, since •OOH was the only intermediate for the electrochemical production of H2O2 via the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction pathway [51,52]. Therefore, the binding energy of O2 and •OOH and the bond length of O2 were calculated, as shown in Table 2
. The doping of boron into carbon material could destroy the original electric neutrality and lead to the rearrangement of electrons of adjacent carbon atoms. Therefore, the binding energies of O2 on the surface of Gr-B (-1.74 eV) and Gr-B-OH (-1.75 eV) were significantly higher than those on the surface of Gr (-0.13 eV) and Gr-B-2OH (-0.41 eV), proving that the surface of Gr-B and Gr-B-OH could promote the adsorption of O2, which was conducive to the electrochemical oxygen reduction process. This was consistent with the report that boron modification was beneficial to the reduction of O2 molecules [53,54]. Compared with the initial state of Gr (1.233 Å), the O-O bond length of Gr-B (1.216 Å) and Gr-B-OH (1.217 Å) was shorter, proving that it was liable to two-electron process. On the contrary, Gr-B-2OH (1.249 Å) was liable to four-electron process. •OOH was more likely to form C-O bond on carbon atoms, which was adjacent to boron. Because carbon was more electronegative than boron, and oxygen reduction reaction was more likely to occur in electron-rich center. More positive •OOH binding energy was favorable to the two-electron process. •OOH had relatively positive binding energy on the surface of Gr, Gr-B, Gr-B-OH, and Gr-B-2OH, which was conducive to desorption of •OOH. It was because that the difference in the electronegativity between B and C was smaller, and the neighboring carbon had weaker adsorption of •OOH. However, the activity of oxygen reduction reaction decreased on the surface of Gr-B and Gr-B-OH, due to the less adsorption energy of O2 and insufficient oxygen adsorption. Therefore, -BC3 and -BC2O groups were conducive to the two-electron process of oxygen reduction reaction.

As shown in the experimental and DFT calculation results, the excellent H2O2 production performance of BAC catalyst could be attributed to the content of boron and the doping groups of boron. The doping of boron into the carbon material could destroy the original electric neutrality and lead to the rearrangement of electrons of adjacent carbon atoms. Thus, the active site of the oxygen reduction reaction was increased, and the oxygen reduction activity of the catalyst was enhanced. On the other hand, -BC3 and -BCO2 groups could not only enhance the adsorption of oxygen, but also promote the formation of •OOH intermediate product, which was conducive to the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction and the increase in the selectivity of the BAC catalyst.

Phenol was applied as the target pollutant to evaluate the degradation performance of BAC-3 catalyst, as shown in Fig. 8
a. As a comparison, the phenol degradation of BAC-3 catalyst was investigated in the oxygen-free and oxygen-standard electrolyte. In the oxygen-free electrolyte, phenol was only removed by 3% after 30 min, indicating that anodic oxidation was not the main reason of phenol degradation. In the oxygen-standard electrolyte, phenol was almost completely removed. It was speculated that BAC catalyst could rapidly degrade phenol without the addition of Fe2+. In order to further verify its mechanism, phenol was degraded by AC catalyst in the oxygen-standard electrolyte without adding iron ion. It was found that phenol was degraded only 43% after 30 min. The reaction rate (0.018 min−1) was much lower than that of phenol degradation of BAC-3 catalyst (0.193 min−1) under the same conditions. It was speculated that BAC catalyst could decompose H2O2 into •OH, so it could degrade phenol rapidly. In the oxygen-standard electrolyte together with the addition of 0.06 mM Fe2+, phenol was degraded by AC catalyst. After 30 min, phenol was degraded by 99%, and the reaction rate (0.104 min−1) was slightly lower than that of BAC-3 catalyst (0.193 min−1). It indicated that the doping with boron into the catalyst could not only enhance the oxygen reduction activity, but also promote the decomposition of H2O2 to form •OH, which was conducive to the occurrence of oxygen reduction reaction. As shown in Fig. S6a, the above reactions all followed first-order reaction kinetics.

In order to further verify that the modification of boron promoted the decomposition of H2O2 to produce •OH, tert-butyl alcohol was added to the solution as the •OH capturing agent. As shown in Figs. 8
b and S6b, the degradation rate of phenol decreased significantly with the addition of tert-butanol alcohol and the above reactions all followed first-order reaction kinetics, which also proved the generation of •OH due to the formation of electron-rich and electron-poor centers. In the vicinity of the rich electronic center, H2O2 was effectively decomposed into •OH, while the electron-poor center captured electrons from the adsorbed pollutants and transferred them to the electron-rich center, thus promoting the efficient degradation of pollutants [26]. Since BAC catalysts could decompose H2O2 into active products, the theoretical yield value of H2O2 should be higher ( Fig. 4 ).

The change curve of phenol concentration over time at different potentials and the linear relation diagram obtained by pseudo-first order kinetics respectively were shown in Figs. 8
c and S6c. The degradation rate of phenol increased gradually with the decline of potential. The kinetic constants of phenol degradation were 0.11 min−1, 0.13 min−1, 0.19 min−1 and 0.28 min−1 at the potential of 0.2 V vs. RHE, 0 V vs. RHE, -0.2 V vs. RHE and -0.4 V vs. RHE, respectively. At a more negative potential, the degradation rate of phenol was faster, due to the higher yield of H2O2.

Figs. 8
d and S6d showed the change curve of phenol concentration over time at different initial pH value and the linear relation diagram obtained by pseudo-first order kinetics. The kinetic constants of phenol degradation were 0.24 min−1, 0.19 min−1, 0.17 min−1 and 0.11 min−1 at the initial pH value of 1, 3, 5 and 7, respectively. The kinetic constant of phenol degradation decreased with the initial pH value increased. Because the production rate of H2O2 in acidic solution was fast, more H2O2 could be provided for electrochemical advanced oxidation reaction and more •OH could be produced for the degradation of pollutants. Moreover, H2O2 was prone to self-decompose into O2 and H2O at the pH above 3.

Stability was also an important index to evaluate the performance of BAC catalysts. Fig. 9
showed the curve of BAC-3 catalyst for degrading phenol cycling 20 times. After 20 cycles, phenol could still be rapidly degraded by BAC-3 catalyst and its degradation rate decreased slightly. Fig. S7 showed the XPS spectra of the electrode with 20 cycles. Compared with the XPS spectra before electrolysis (-BC3 34%, -BC2O 54%, -BCO2 12%), the proportion of boron groups after electrolysis (-BC3 30%, -BC2O 61%, -BCO2 9%) was basically the same. The above results proved that BAC catalyst had better stability.

Generally, the selective reduction of O2 and the rapid generation of •OH enhanced the electrochemical advanced oxidation performance of BAC cathode. The electron distribution in the original carbon plane was destroyed because of the introduction of boron, thus enhancing the oxygen reduction performance. In addition, the adsorption capacity of intermediate products on the catalyst surface was weaker in oxygen reduction reaction, which was conducive to the generation of H2O2, because the electronegativity difference between boron and carbon atoms was small. Therefore, BAC cathode was superior to other electrodes in aspects of oxygen reduction capacity and selectivity. It was worth noting that the BAC cathode could quickly generate •OH without adding Fe2+, which accelerated the degradation of phenol. It overcame the disadvantage of secondary pollution caused by introducing metal ions during the traditional electro-Fenton process. Therefore, the in-situ electrocatalysis on BAC could be regarded as an environment-friendly pollutant removal system without risk of metal contamination. A further work needed to be done to explore the mechanism about promoting the decomposition of H2O2 into •OH by boron element. It was believed that these studies were conducive to the development of electrocatalyst for two-electron oxygen reduction process. This work also contributed to achieving rapid degradation of industrial persistent organic pollutants.

In this paper, BAC containing different boron groups content was prepared as low-cost and efficient catalysts. BAC possessed a more positive onset potential of oxygen reduction reaction (0.458 V vs. RHE) and a faster rate of H2O2 generation (365 mg/L pH=3). DFT calculations showed that -BC3 and -BC2O active groups not only enhanced oxygen adsorption and accelerated oxygen activation, but also shortened the O-O bond length during oxygen adsorption. Meanwhile, a small electronegativity difference between B and O facilitated the desorption of •OOH. The XPS results confirmed the above results, more -BC3 and -BC2O active groups were conducive to the generation of H2O2. The rate of in-situ phenol degradation (0.193 min−1) increased by an order of magnitude, compared with activated carbon (0.018 min−1), because BAC promoted the in-situ conversion of H2O2 to •OH. Moreover, the in-situ catalysis system overcame the disadvantages of the traditional electro-Fenton process, which not only enlarged working pH range, but also prevented secondary pollution. After 20 cycles, phenol can still be rapidly degraded by BAC-3 catalyst, which proved that BAC catalyst had excellent stability. Overall, this work provided theoretical guidance for the further application of boron catalyst for two-electron oxygen reduction process.

Xu Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Lida Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Wen Sun: Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhengqing Yang: Writing – review & editing. Jingjing Jin: Writing – review & editing. Danni You: Writing – review & editing. Guichang Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.139462.
